gold_final_answer: a) A must equal its transpose. b) Example: the matrix whose only nonzero entries are ones in the first row at the first and second columns (all other entries zero) satisfies the property but is not symmetric.
prompt:
Let $n \ge 3$ be an integer number. We say that a matrix $A \in \mathcal{M}_n(\mathbb{C})$ has the property $(\mathcal{P})$ if $\det(A + X_{ij}) = \det(A + X_{ji})$, for any $i, j \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$, where $X_{ij} \in \mathcal{M}_n(\mathbb{C})$ is the matrix with $1$ at the position $(i, j)$ and $0$ elsewhere.

a) Assume a matrix $A \in \mathcal{M}_n(\mathbb{C})$ with the property $(\mathcal{P})$, such that $\det(A) \neq 0$. Prove that $A = A^T$.

b) Give an example of a matrix $A \in \mathcal{M}_n(\mathbb{C})$ with the property $(\mathcal{P})$, but $A \neq A^T$.
response_text:
Let \(E_{ij}=X_{ij}\) denote the matrix with a single \(1\) in position \((i,j)\).

We use the standard fact:

\[
\det(A+tE_{ij})=\det(A)+t\,C_{ij}(A),
\]

where \(C_{ij}(A)\) is the cofactor of the entry \((i,j)\) of \(A\).  
Indeed, the determinant is linear in each entry, and only the \((i,j)\)-entry is changed.

So, for \(t=1\),

\[
\det(A+E_{ij})=\det(A)+C_{ij}(A).
\]

Hence property \((\mathcal P)\) means

\[
\det(A)+C_{ij}(A)=\det(A)+C_{ji}(A)\qquad \forall i,j,
\]

thus

\[
C_{ij}(A)=C_{ji}(A)\qquad \forall i,j.
\]

Therefore the cofactor matrix \(C(A)\) is symmetric.

Now recall that

\[
\operatorname{adj}(A)=C(A)^T.
\]

So if \(C(A)\) is symmetric, then \(\operatorname{adj}(A)\) is symmetric as well.

Also, when \(\det(A)\neq 0\),

\[
A^{-1}=\frac{1}{\det(A)}\operatorname{adj}(A).
\]

Thus \(A^{-1}\) is symmetric. Taking transpose,

\[
(A^{-1})^T=A^{-1}.
\]

But \((A^T)^{-1}=(A^{-1})^T=A^{-1}\), hence \(A^T=A\). This proves part (a).

---

### b) Example with \((\mathcal P)\) but not symmetric

Take any singular matrix of rank \(\le n-2\). Then all \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) minors vanish, so every cofactor is \(0\). Hence for all \(i,j\),

\[
\det(A+E_{ij})=\det(A)+C_{ij}(A)=\det(A).
\]

Therefore \((\mathcal P)\) holds automatically.

So it suffices to choose a non-symmetric matrix of rank \(\le n-2\). For example, for any \(n\ge 3\),

\[
A=E_{12}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1&0&\cdots&0\\
0&0&0&\cdots&0\\
0&0&0&\cdots&0\\
\vdots&\vdots&\vdots&&\vdots\\
0&0&0&\cdots&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

This matrix is not symmetric, and its rank is \(1\le n-2\). Hence all cofactors vanish, so \(A\) has property \((\mathcal P)\), but \(A\ne A^T\).

Final answer:  
a) From \(\det(A+E_{ij})=\det(A)+C_{ij}(A)\), property \((\mathcal P)\) gives \(C_{ij}(A)=C_{ji}(A)\) for all \(i,j\), so the cofactor matrix is symmetric. Hence \(\operatorname{adj}(A)\) is symmetric, and if \(\det(A)\neq0\), then \(A^{-1}=\frac1{\det(A)}\operatorname{adj}(A)\) is symmetric. Therefore \(A=(A^T)\), i.e. \(A=A^T\).  
b) A valid example is \(A=E_{12}\). It is not symmetric, and since \(\operatorname{rank}(A)=1\le n-2\), all cofactors vanish, so \(\det(A+E_{ij})=\det(A+E_{ji})\) for all \(i,j\).
